title: The ethics of leaving an organisation that needs you
summary: Argue whether professionals have a moral duty to stay in a struggling organisation, or whether leaving is a legitimate and even necessary choice.
topic: The ethics of leaving an organisation that needs you
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) exploring whether someone has a moral obligation to stay in an organisation — a hospital, a school, a non-profit, a government agency — that genuinely needs them, even when better opportunities are available elsewhere. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete case (a country with a doctor shortage, a rural school losing its only science teacher, an NGO mid-crisis). Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What is often framed as personal disloyalty is, in most cases, nothing more than…
Admittedly, there are situations in which a single departure can destabilise an entire…
Having invested years in building a team's capacity, a departing professional carries with them…

không thể thiếu được

dùng cảm giác tội lỗi để ép buộc ai đó
quyền tự chủ nghề nghiệp
đồng lõa trong

sự thất bại mang tính hệ thống
bị dàn trải quá mức / kiệt sức vì quá nhiều việc
lợi dụng thiện chí

hintVocab.phrase: to vest responsibility in an individual
hintVocab.meaningVi: đặt trách nhiệm lên vai một cá nhân
bình thường hóa điều kiện tồi tệ
lộ trình / quỹ đạo sự nghiệp
bị ràng buộc bởi
từ bỏ trách nhiệm
kế hoạch kế nhiệm
sự phục vụ tự nguyện không có đền bù xứng đáng

sampleText:
What is often framed as personal disloyalty is, in most cases, nothing more than a rational response to conditions that an organisation's leadership created and has failed to address. When a surgeon leaves a chronically underfunded district hospital in rural Zambia for a better-equipped post in Lusaka, she is not abandoning her patients — she is refusing to absorb, indefinitely, the cost of a systemic failure that belongs to health authorities, not to her.

The argument that skilled professionals are morally bound to remain in struggling institutions rests on a sleight of hand: it vests structural responsibility in individuals while leaving the institutions themselves unaccountable. A rural school in the Philippines that loses its only chemistry teacher does not lose her because she is disloyal; it loses her because no mechanism exists to retain staff, offer development, or replace them. Blaming the departing teacher normalises the conditions that drove her out and relieves the system of any pressure to change.

Admittedly, there are situations in which a single departure can destabilise an entire team. An NGO managing food distribution during a crisis in northern Ethiopia, for instance, may genuinely depend on one logistics coordinator whose institutional knowledge cannot be quickly transferred. In such cases, a professional who leaves with minimal notice is making a choice that has real consequences for real people, and that deserves honest acknowledgement.

Even so, the expectation that individuals must sacrifice career progression indefinitely — simply because organisations have been allowed to become dependent on them — is a form of institutional exploitation. Having spent years building expertise that the market now rewards elsewhere, a professional who stays is effectively subsidising a management failure. The ethical response to that dependency is better succession planning and structural reform, not guilt.

The broader danger of insisting that individuals stay is that it preserves a system in which poor conditions are treated as acceptable precisely because someone is always willing to endure them. Each professional who remains in a role they have outgrown, in an institution that underpays and overworks them, makes it easier for those in authority to defer the reforms that would make the role sustainable for the next person. Leaving, in that sense, can be the more honest act: it forces a reckoning that loyalty quietly prevents.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to reframe the charge of disloyalty
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What is often framed as personal disloyalty is … nothing more than a rational response' dùng cấu trúc 'What X is Y' để đặt lại vấn đề: thay vì chấp nhận định nghĩa 'bất trung', tác giả định nghĩa lại hành động đó là 'phản ứng hợp lý'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What is often framed as personal disloyalty is … nothing more than a rational response' uses a what-cleft to contest the framing: rather than accepting 'disloyalty' as the right description, the writer substitutes 'rational response', placing their reframing in the strongest syntactic position.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Participle clause to show accumulated context
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having spent years building expertise that the market now rewards elsewhere' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành ('Having + V3') để nén một khoảng thời gian dài vào một cụm từ, nhấn mạnh rằng sự cống hiến đã diễn ra trước khi quyết định ra đi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having spent years building expertise that the market now rewards elsewhere' uses a perfect participle clause to compress a long prior period into a single phrase, establishing that the professional's contribution came first — making the case against any remaining obligation more forceful.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều thường được đóng khung là sự bất trung cá nhân, trong hầu hết các trường hợp, không hơn gì một phản ứng hợp lý trước những điều kiện mà ban lãnh đạo tổ chức đã tạo ra và không giải quyết được. Khi một bác sĩ phẫu thuật rời bỏ một bệnh viện huyện thiếu kinh phí mãn tính ở vùng nông thôn Zambia để đến một vị trí được trang bị tốt hơn ở Lusaka, cô ấy không phải đang bỏ rơi bệnh nhân của mình — cô ấy đang từ chối gánh chịu, vô thời hạn, cái giá của một thất bại mang tính hệ thống thuộc về cơ quan y tế, không phải của cô ấy.

Lập luận rằng các chuyên gia có kỹ năng bị ràng buộc về mặt đạo đức phải ở lại trong các tổ chức đang gặp khó khăn dựa trên một thủ thuật đánh lừa: nó đặt trách nhiệm cơ cấu lên vai cá nhân trong khi để các tổ chức thoát khỏi trách nhiệm giải trình. Một trường học nông thôn ở Philippines mất đi giáo viên hóa học duy nhất không phải vì cô ấy không trung thành; họ mất cô ấy vì không có cơ chế nào để giữ chân nhân viên, tạo cơ hội phát triển, hoặc thay thế họ. Đổ lỗi cho giáo viên ra đi bình thường hóa những điều kiện đã đẩy cô ấy đi và giải phóng hệ thống khỏi mọi áp lực thay đổi.

Thừa nhận, có những tình huống mà một lần ra đi có thể làm bất ổn cả một đội nhóm. Một tổ chức phi chính phủ quản lý phân phối thực phẩm trong một cuộc khủng hoảng ở miền bắc Ethiopia, chẳng hạn, có thể thực sự phụ thuộc vào một điều phối viên hậu cần mà kiến thức tổ chức của họ không thể chuyển giao nhanh chóng. Trong những trường hợp như vậy, một chuyên gia ra đi với thông báo tối thiểu đang đưa ra một lựa chọn có hậu quả thực sự cho những người thực, và điều đó xứng đáng được thừa nhận thành thật.

Dẫu vậy, kỳ vọng rằng các cá nhân phải hy sinh sự thăng tiến nghề nghiệp vô thời hạn — đơn giản vì các tổ chức đã được phép trở nên phụ thuộc vào họ — là một hình thức bóc lột tổ chức. Sau khi dành nhiều năm xây dựng chuyên môn mà thị trường hiện tưởng thưởng ở nơi khác, một chuyên gia ở lại thực chất đang trợ cấp cho một thất bại quản lý. Phản ứng đạo đức với sự phụ thuộc đó là lập kế hoạch kế nhiệm tốt hơn và cải cách cơ cấu, không phải cảm giác tội lỗi.

Nguy hiểm rộng lớn hơn của việc khăng khăng các cá nhân phải ở lại là nó bảo tồn một hệ thống trong đó các điều kiện tồi tệ được coi là chấp nhận được chính xác vì luôn có người sẵn sàng chịu đựng chúng. Mỗi chuyên gia ở lại trong một vai trò họ đã vượt qua, trong một tổ chức trả lương thấp và làm họ quá tải, giúp những người có thẩm quyền dễ dàng trì hoãn các cải cách mà sẽ làm cho vai trò đó bền vững cho người tiếp theo. Ra đi, theo nghĩa đó, có thể là hành động trung thực hơn: nó buộc phải đối mặt với thực tế mà lòng trung thành lặng lẽ ngăn chặn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to reframe the accusation of disloyalty?
options: What is often framed as personal disloyalty is, in most cases, nothing more than a rational response to conditions that an organisation's leadership created.
It is personal disloyalty that is rational response conditions leadership created.
The disloyalty what is framed as personal is rational response nothing more.
What rational response is framed as disloyalty conditions leadership created.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft 'What X is Y' places the writer's reframing — 'rational response' — in the most emphatic position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's preferred term at the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a perfect participle clause correctly?
options: Having spent years building expertise that the market now rewards elsewhere, a professional who stays is effectively subsidising a management failure.
Spent having years building expertise market rewards elsewhere professional stays subsidising.
Years having been spent building expertise elsewhere professional stays subsidise management.
A professional having years spent building expertise is subsidising stays market rewards.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having + past participle' clause establishes prior action; its subject must match the main clause subject ('a professional').
mcQuestions.hint: The participle clause subject must be the same as the main clause subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces a concession most naturally?
options: In conclusion, single departures can destabilise teams.
Admittedly, there are situations in which a single departure can destabilise an entire team.
Despite this single departures destabilise teams admittedly.
Even though admittedly single departures destabilise entire teams.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly, …' cleanly admits the opposing case before the essay pivots back to the main argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the single-word opener that most naturally concedes a point.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'vests structural responsibility in individuals' mean?
options: Gives individuals expensive uniforms paid for by the organisation
Formally places responsibility that belongs to the system onto individual people
Invests financial resources in the personal development of employees
Creates legal contracts binding individuals to the organisation
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To vest X in Y' means to formally place or assign X to Y; here, responsibility that belongs to the system is misplaced onto individuals.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'vest authority/responsibility in someone' means legally or formally.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the ethical response to institutional dependency on a single person?
options: Asking that person to sign a contract committing them to stay
Appealing to their professional loyalty and sense of duty
Better succession planning and structural reform
Paying them slightly more to discourage them from leaving
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'The ethical response to that dependency is better succession planning and structural reform, not guilt.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence beginning 'The ethical response to that dependency is…'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly focuses on the person affected rather than the agent?
options: No mechanism exists to retain staff, offer development, or replace them.
No mechanism retaining exists staff offer development replace them.
They retain no mechanism existing staff development replace.
Staff are retained no mechanism existing offer development.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No mechanism exists to retain staff' uses an impersonal construction that foregrounds the absence of support rather than blaming anyone directly.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option makes the systemic gap (no mechanism) the grammatical subject?

options: A medical condition caused by overwork
The loss of skilled professionals from a place or organisation to somewhere better
A technique used to empty an organisation's archives
The process of training new employees to replace retiring ones
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Brain drain' is the established term for the emigration of skilled or educated people from one place to another, usually in search of better conditions.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Brain' here refers to intellectual talent — where does that talent go?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'normalise' in its metaphorical sense?
options: Blaming the departing teacher normalises the conditions that drove her out.
The teacher normalises conditions blaming departing drove her out.
Conditions that drove her out are normalising teacher departing blame.
Normalise conditions drove her out blaming teacher departing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To normalise' means to make something seem normal or acceptable; the subject (blaming) causes the conditions to be treated as routine.
mcQuestions.hint: Subject + 'normalises' + object is the correct structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best expresses 'working beyond one's sustainable capacity'?
options: career trajectory
succession planning
being stretched thin
institutional dependency
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stretched thin' is the idiomatic phrase for someone or something that is spread across too many demands and has no remaining capacity.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of elastic being pulled beyond its limit — which phrase uses that image?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which of the following best summarises the essay's main argument?
options: Professionals should always prioritise personal career growth over any commitment to their organisation.
Leaving a struggling organisation is sometimes justified, but only if you give long advance notice.
The moral burden of an institution's problems belongs to the institution, not to individual professionals who choose to leave.
Skilled professionals are obligated to stay until a suitable replacement has been fully trained.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay consistently argues that systemic failures are structural problems, not individual moral failures — leaving is a rational, defensible response.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places the responsibility on the institution rather than on the individual?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to exploit goodwill' mean in a professional context?
options: To use someone's willingness to help as a way of avoiding proper support or payment
To write a positive reference letter for a departing colleague
To negotiate a higher salary using competing job offers
To take on additional responsibilities voluntarily
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Exploit goodwill' means using someone's cooperative, willing attitude as a substitute for proper resources, pay, or structural support.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Goodwill' is a positive, cooperative attitude — what does it mean to exploit that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'effectively' as a hedging adverb?
options: A professional who stays is effectively subsidising a management failure.
Effectively a professional subsidising stays management failure is.
A professional stays effectively subsidising is management failure.
Management failure effectively stays professional is subsidising.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Effectively' softens the claim by suggesting this is the practical outcome — without stating it is the stated intention.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Effectively' meaning 'in practice, even if not officially' should sit before the main verb phrase.

options: To carry out reforms ahead of schedule
To cancel reforms permanently
To postpone making changes that are needed
To propose reforms to a committee for approval
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Defer' means to postpone; 'defer reforms' means to keep putting off necessary changes.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Defer' shares a root with 'deferred payment' — what does it mean to delay something?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a pivot back to the main argument after a concession?
options: In conclusion,
Granted,
Even so, the expectation that individuals must sacrifice career progression indefinitely is a form of institutional exploitation.
For example,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges that the concession was valid and then pivots firmly to the essay's main claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option both acknowledges what was just admitted and returns forcefully to the main point?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a reckoning' mean in 'it forces a reckoning that loyalty quietly prevents'?
options: A financial audit of the organisation's accounts
A confrontation with difficult truths or consequences that have been avoided
A formal legal judgment against the organisation
A ceremony recognising the contributions of a departing employee
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A reckoning' means being forced to deal with the consequences of something — often truths that have been suppressed or deferred.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Reckoning' often follows 'day of' — it means facing something you have been avoiding.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains formal, academic register throughout?
options: It's so unfair to tell doctors they have to stay in bad hospitals just because no one else will.
Like, the whole guilt-trip thing organisations do is honestly just toxic and people need to stop.
The expectation that individuals must sacrifice career progression indefinitely is a form of institutional exploitation.
Doctors and teachers should just leave tbh because the system is broken anyway.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise, formal vocabulary ('sacrifice career progression', 'institutional exploitation') and complete syntactic structures appropriate for academic writing.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid contractions, slang, and colloquial intensifiers like 'so', 'just', 'tbh'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever stayed in a role, a team, or a commitment longer than was good for you because you felt others needed you? Looking back, who was that choice really serving?
